  • Focus on a Single PLAN Version: Using a single PLAN Version ensures planners always know where to input their data. This reduces confusion and eliminates the administrative burden of maintaining multiple editable Versions.

  • Pre-Configured Input Forms in Rapid FP&A: Input forms in the Rapid FP&A Suite are pre-configured to filter on the PLAN Version, ensuring planners work in the "Working Plan" Version.

  • Leverage Data Actions: The Rapid FP&A Suite includes pre-configured Data Actions that allow you to copy any PLAN data into finalized, version-specific names like Target2025 or Budget2024. Once copied, these versions become read-only and are naturally referred to by their specific names (e.g., Forecast2025) for clarity and consistency.

Why does this approach work? It focuses planners on completing one version of the Plan before moving to another, avoiding the complexity and errors of managing multiple editable versions simultaneously.

Managing Data Within Private Versions

Private Versions offer a flexible environment to refine and validate planning scenarios before publishing to Public Versions. Here are some best practices:

How to Use Private Versions Effectively

Planners must flow their plan inputs through Private Versions, regardless of how Public Versions are managed, so educating Planners on how to use Private Versions optimally is crucial. Proper use of Private Versions ensures accurate updates to the plan and provides  flexibility:

  • Experiment and Refine: Use Private Versions to model scenarios, refine your inputs, and validate changes without impacting others.

  • Publish with Purpose: Once all updates are finalized, publish the changes to the Public Version to make them available for organizational use. Under the Rapid FP&A Suite, published updates flow into the PLAN Version (Working Plan), which admins can later archive into specific Version names (e.g., Budget2026) once all plans are collected.

  • Utilize Tools for Confidence: Features like the “Revert” function and “Version History Panel” provide a safety net to undo mistakes during refinement of your Private version. Remember: Reverting is unavailable after publishing it in the Public Version.

Tip: The Rapid FP&A Suite includes pre-configured input forms and sample data, allowing you to practice updating and publishing Private Versions in a "practice" environment.

Best Practices for Managing Public Versions

Creating multiple specific Version names (e.g., Budget2026, Forecast2025) at the start of every planning cycle can be problematic. This approach requires updating all input forms and reports to align with each new Version name, creating inefficiencies and potential confusion. Additionally, frequent changes to Version names can leave users unsure where to input their plan data, causing unnecessary delays and errors.

Use Categories to Organize Your Versions

You can use the category property to organize your versions by type. Categories help distinguish versions and streamline management. The following categories can help organize versions:

  • Actual, Budget, Plan, Forecast, Rolling Forecast

A model can have one public Actuals version, typically updated via import from a data source like S/4HANA. However, private copies of this version can be created for analysis or testing. Categories like Forecast allow you to create multiple forecast versions to explore optimistic, baseline, or worst-case outcomes.

Under Best Practices in the Rapid FP&A Suite, planners will focus on one scenario at a time (e.g., baseline) within the PLAN Version. Once the scenario is finalized, it can be archived under a version-specific name such as Baseline2025 or Optimistic2025, and then the planner can move on to the following scenario. This approach ensures planners complete one scenario before moving to another, avoiding confusion and streamlining the process.

Hold Actual Data in a Read-Only Version

  • Store finalized Actual data in a read-only ACTUAL Version. This ensures the integrity of historical data and provides a clear baseline for variance analysis.

Archive Specific Versions for Reporting

  • Instead of constantly updating input forms and security for new write-enabled Versions, take snapshots of the working PLAN Version at key points in the planning cycle. Archive these snapshots into finalized, read-only Versions (e.g., Budget2026, Forecast2025) using pre-built Data Actions from the Rapid FP&A Suite.

  • This approach preserves the integrity of snapshot data while avoiding the repetitive task of reconfiguring templates and security for every user or group needing write access to a plan version.

How Rapid FP&A Suite Supports Version Management

The Rapid FP&A Suite for SAP S/4HANA is a valuable toolset for:

  1. Testing and Experimentation

    • Use sample data to explore how updates to Private Versions flow into Public Versions.

  2. Streamlined Workflows

    • Discover how we maintain a single write-enabled PLAN Version for all planning activities.

    • See how admins can create finalized, locked-down Versions efficiently by copying a version from PLAN

  3. Reducing Administrative Overhead

    • You will see how pre-configured input forms align with the active working PLAN Version.

    • With only one active PLAN version all the time, you simplify security and configuration management

  4. Preserving Data Integrity

    • Finalized Versions like Budget2026 or Forecast2025 are clearly labeled and locked for accurate reporting.

    • Any other interim snapshots of PLAN can be archived for reviews or status meetings.

Benefits for FP&A Teams?

  • Planners: Clarity on where to input data with a single active Version.

  • Admins: Efficiency in managing Versions with reduced maintenance effort.

  • Stakeholders: Reliable, finalized Versions ready for variance analysis and decision-making.

Getting Started

Ready to unlock the full potential of Version Management? Leverage the Rapid FP&A Suite for SAP S/4HANA as a toolset to test and refine your planning processes. Explore its pre-configured content, including input forms, data actions, and sample scenarios, to simplify and standardize your workflows. The best part? You can download the suite in minutes and review these processes using sample data—no integration is required during your discovery phase.

How do you Download the free Rapid FP&A Suite?
Downloading the Rapid FP&A Suite is straightforward and hassle-free.
Follow these steps to get started:
1. Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud account.   Please don't use a production tenant.
2. Navigate to the "Content Network" on the left-hand side panel of SAC
3. Select "Business Content"
4. Search for "Rapid" in the upper right-hand 'Search' box - you should see Rapid Finance, Workforce, Sales
4. Follow the instructions to complete the download and installation of each of the Rapid modules (Rapid Finance, Rapid Sales, Rapid Workforce).

Let’s make planning Rapid, Simple, and Standard with SAP Analytics Cloud!

entry.title: Details of SAP Data and Analytics Advisory Methodology Phase IV (Data Governance & Roadmaps)
entry.content:
In the previous blogs, I have provided details of the first three phases of the methodology for developing a target architecture for data-driven business outcomes:

Phase I: Scoping & Baseline Analysis
Phase II: Business Outcomes & Solution Requirements
Phase III: Capability Map & Solution Architecture

This blog is about the final phase, with focus on data governance and roadmap planning.

Overview of phase IV

Phase IV concludes your exploration of a new target architecture by assessing your data governance and organizational aspects and creating a roadmap or project plan for implementing the new architecture.

In the first two steps of this phase, you investigate relevant data governance and organizational aspects by applying the “Data and Analytics Maturity Assessment”. This topic is important as it is a key enabler to successfully establish the chosen architecture und realize the business outcomes defined in phase II.

Step 3 of this phase describes how to realize the data & analytics solution architecture by consolidating and planning all required activities and creating a high-level roadmap or project plan.

Steps IV.1 & 2: Data Governance / Roles & Organization

I have already discussed the details of the “Data and Analytics Maturity Assessment” in the previous blog. It is used to assess the maturity of data governance (step IV.1) and organizational aspects (step IV.2) and to derive the necessary improvement actions from the gaps between the current and desired maturity levels.

The activities identified during the assessment are consolidated and made available for the final step, roadmap planning.

Here is an example:

Step IV.3: Roadmaps/Project Plan

A roadmap is a strategic planning tool designed to align short-term and long-term goals with specific IT solutions. It presents these plans in formats, such as a sunray diagram or a planning chart.

An architecture roadmap organizes activities based on business needs and priorities to achieve the target architecture within a defined time frame. These activities are grouped into functional clusters to streamline planning and execution.

You start by collecting the activities to realize the defined solution architecture in an activity catalog. Each activity is defined with a name, description, and owner. Next, you review the recommended actions from the Data & Analytics Maturity Assessment, break them down into more detailed activities if necessary and add them into the activity catalog.

Then, you organize the activities according to logical clusters. You can define clusters by grouping activities that belong to the same context.

Examples of such clusters include:

  • Architecture Validation (PoC)
  • Implementation
  • Operations
  • Security
  • Know-How Transfer
  • Organization and Governance

Next, you order the activities and identify dependencies to other activities. Finally, you estimate a high-level timeline.

Here is an example of an activity catalogue:

The roadmap is then created based on the activity catalog. The following example shows the roadmap in a sunray diagram.

This completes the Data & Analytics Advisory Methodology. The next step could be the preparation of an implementation project / program where the roadmap is thoroughly planned, resources are assigned, and the architecture design is detailed out.

This article concludes the blog series that introduced the methodology and detailed the phases and steps. But this is not the end,…

…as the methodology is now available in the SAP Help Portal. It provides a comprehensive guidance on how to apply the methodology and includes the content and templates that are provided in the SAP Workzone.

We appreciate any feedback on the methodology and its documentation. We are especially interested in hearing about your experiences using the methodology. Please contact us by e-mail at sap-data-analytics-methodology@sap.com.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud tenants on Quarterly Release Cycle (QRC) updates in 2025
entry.content:
Upcoming enhancements in SAP Analytics Cloud for the year 2025 can be accessed through the Road Map page.

Explore the latest features, highlights and updates from our recent releases on the page  https://www.sap.com/products/cloud-analytics/features/release-highlights.html .

If you're seeking information on release dates from the preceding year, 2024, you can refer to the details provided in this Blog Post.

SAP Analytics Cloud aligns with SAP’s global strategy for cloud application releases, following SAP Cloud products 2025 calendar:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP Ariba
  • SAP Business Network (incl. SAP Business Network for Procurement, SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration, and SAP Business Network for Logistics)
  • SAP BTP, ABAP environment
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (see exceptions)
  • SAP Concur
  • SAP Customer Experience* (see exceptions)
  • SAP Enterprise Asset Management
  • SAP Fieldglass
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning
  • SAP Manufacturing
  • SAP Product Lifecycle Management
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) (see exceptions)
  • SAP SuccessFactors (see exceptions)

*Excludes SAP Commerce Cloud releases

Customer-impacting releases may only take place in February, May, August and November each year.

 

Following each update, upon logging into SAP Analytics Cloud, you will encounter a prominent notification banner featuring a link to our informative "What’s New" help content. You will see Help page of What’s New-2024 Releases.

 

SAP Harmonized Quarterly Updates for 2025 are the following:

  • 2025 Q1: Feb 14-16 (2025.01)
  • 2025 Q2: May 16-18 (2025.08)
  • 2025 Q3: Aug 15-17 (2025.14)
  • 2025 Q4: Nov 14-16 (2025.21)

For more details, see KBA 2888562.

SAP Analytics Cloud QRC release calendar 2025

 

SAP Analytics Cloud, as all cloud products, may have an optional, no-impact release on harmonized monthly feature delivery weekends.

As with the quarterly releases, each product has the option to release during any or all of these windows. Customers should not need to prepare for or take action for the monthly feature deliveries.

 

Note: Content can only be moved between systems on different release cycles at the time of a quarterly release cycle update.

To transport using Content Network: The source and destination systems need to be on the same quarterly version of SAP Analytics Cloud, or the following quarterly version. If the importing system is on an older version, the package won’t appear. If it’s two or more versions ahead of the exporting system, the package’s content may not be supported.

Using the Transport (file) Method (zipped / .TGZ files) – Content can only be imported in the same version of the application from which it was exported, or the subsequent version. After your system is upgraded again, you cannot import the content.

entry.title: Dashboard Design - Science & Standards, Not Random Charts & Art. (Part One)
entry.content:
• Part One - Context
  • Part Two - Chart Types
  • Part Three - Standardisation
  • Part Four - Not sure yet

"I went to the casino yesterday & today I have €25,000 in my account"

Excellent result, or is it? Every day, I observe the content our customers create and notice a prevalent issue in a lot of dashboard designs - context. What does this mean? Simply put, a number on a dashboard remains just that - a number. Is it an improvement from last month, does it surpass the target, or is it below our expectations?

With no context there is no insight and if I had £1 for every time I seen a dashboard showing nothing more than a plain number I would retire. What does a number tell you without context - nothing, but we continue to create content that does not include context, and we think this is good design. Let's take the above example and look at some visualisations for this data:

Chart #1 provides a basic view of the balance without much additional insight beyond the figure. While it might suffice for those familiar with the expected daily balance, in practice, this lacks practicality and can be deemed as poorly designed. Contrastingly, Charts #2 and #3 offer a fresh perspective on the balance, especially when considered alongside the budget details outlined in Table #4.

The importance of context is highlighted here - each chart's value is magnified when accompanied by relevant information. Contextual clarity within dashboards is crucial for effective decision-making, as mere numbers without context may not serve their intended purpose. Let's look at another example:

This excerpt presents a segment from a Sales Dashboard (e-fashion for those that know ) indicating robust sales nearing €22 million, with a detailed breakdown of individual products illustrated in the accompanying bar chart. The overall performance appears strong, particularly highlighting Trousers as one of the top-selling items, generating €3.3 million in sales. However, consider this: what if I informed you that trouser sales were, in fact, the least successful product in our store? Would you find that plausible?

It is a straightforward reality that the dashboard does not provide a clear indication of the company's actual performance. While the numerical data is visible, it lacks significance without the necessary context. Let us examine the same data once more, this time accompanied by additional information.

The narrative is not as favourable as it initially appears, with sales amounting to €22 million, falling short of the target of €23.4 million by nearly €1.4 million. The situation is further exacerbated by the fact that trousers experienced the most significant shortfall, missing their targets by 25% compared to other products. While the data remains unchanged, its lack of context renders it meaningless and potentially misleading.

Accurate presentation of information—including the appropriate chart type, orientation, colours, standards, and context - is essential for deriving meaningful insights from data. In this series of blogs, we will explore each of these elements in greater detail.

It is important to acknowledge that this challenge is one faced by all organizations, even SAP. The following example is based on some SAP Analytics Cloud business content that has recently been overhauled. This earlier version highlights that we too can sometimes display data in a less-than-optimal format.

Yes, there is a little context in the right-hand panel, but the 68,299.63 kWh and 347.82 kWh numbers really tell us very little. I for one don't know if 68,299.63 kWh is high or low and do you know if 43.01 kWh is good for Hourly Max Gas Consumption... you don't? me neither.

It's not just a lack of contextual comparison that can cause problems. Scale is another that we'll cover in more detail in later offerings but check out this great find by Rob Waller (check him out on LinkedIn) regarding scale and information insight.

Source: The Guardian Newspaper - 27th January 2025

The first chart illustrates the significant decline in the market following the news about DeepSeek. In contrast, the second chart reveals that this 'fall' was relatively minor in the grand scheme of things. The context is provided by the scale of the charts; in the first instance, the axis ranges from 19,200 to 20,000, representing only a 5% variation across the entire chart. Conversely, the second chart spans from 14,000 to 20,000, which constitutes a 30% variation, thereby offering a more precise representation of the situation.

Dashboards that lack context merely present data; they do not provide meaningful insights. SAP Analytics Cloud addresses this limitation by employing techniques such as Variances. Users can generate variances based on different versions, such as Target versus Actual or by timeframes, like Current versus Previous Period. These variances offer the insights that raw data alone cannot provide.

The example below illustrates a comparison between our actual sales figures and the target, highlighting the difference (Δ) and utilizing colour coding (to be discussed in a future blog) to indicate performance, whether positive or negative. Only two colours, that's Red and Green are acceptable, but we'll talk about that in the next posting.

We invite you to deepen your understanding through our upcoming events. Andrew and I are pleased to announce that we will be conducting four public visualization webinars in 2025. Registration links will be made available below and will activate after February 12th. We encourage you to check this page regularly for updates. Please be aware that these links will enable you to indicate your interest in participating, and we will notify you of your acceptance status once registration is open.

  • 12th March 2025 - Register your interest here.
  • 13th May 2025 - Register your interest here.
  • 9th September 2025 - Register your interest here.
  • 9th December 2025 - Register your interest here.

Throw your question and comments below and I'll always attempt to answer them as soon as I can.

Happy Story Building

Daniel

Find me on LinkedIn

entry.title: Enhancing Filter Visibility in SAP Analytics Cloud Numeric Pointer Charts
entry.content:
Solution

Add a Text Box to the Story Page: -

You can add this text box to sit on top of the Numeric point chart, you can place it in any position on the story or drag it on top of the chart, as shown below I have placed the Text Box on top of the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: -

Click on the … button, then click on +Add -> Dynamic Text as shown below: -

A dialog box will open called: Insert Dynamic Text. In this box, scroll down and select the required chart that you want to add the Year value to.
Note: You must be careful in identifying the correct Numeric Point chart such that the correct year filter value will be displayed on each respective Numeric Point chart.
Process is shown below: -

a) Here I have selected the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: Operation, because I want to refer to the year value of only this chart.

b) Drill down on the filter section of the chart

c) Select the required filter value, here it is Plan Year.

d) Once you are done with the above, click on Create in Insert Dynamic text box.


The Year value will now start showing on the text box now: -

Note: You can also add some text manually (before & after to the year value) if you so require. Just click on the year value and position your cursor as needed.

For formatting the value in the text box, since this is dynamic text, just select the value and then right click the value in the text box and then select Edit Styling… as shown below: -

You can make multiple filter selections(e.g., on Year) on the Numeric Point Chart and then based on that the Text Box value will change automatically.

entry.title: SAC | Passing URL Parameters from Optimized Story to Data Analyzer
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog, we explore how to seamlessly pass URL parameters from an SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Optimized Story to the SAC Data Analyzer. This integration enhances data exploration capabilities by allowing users to drill down into data at deeper levels based on their specific requirements. Moreover, it enables the SAC Data Analyzer to open in the same state as the story, preserving filters and variables, and thus providing a more fluid and personalized data analysis experience.

Blog Highlights

This blog focuses on:

  • Writing script to pass a user’s selection from an SAC story to the Data Analyzer.
  • Utilizing SAC’s scripting classes to achieve parameter passing.
  • Demonstrating practical use cases for enhanced data exploration.

Benefits of Passing URL Parameters

  1. Enhanced Data Exploration: Users can drill down into data effortlessly, enhancing their ability to uncover insights.
  2. Advanced Features Utilization: The Data Analyzer offers various data exploration features that are readily accessible.
  3. State Preservation: By passing filters and variables, the Data Analyzer can open in the same state as the story, aligning with user needs and context.

Key Scripting Classes

To achieve the parameter passing, we utilize the following scripting classes:

  • NavigationUtils: Facilitates navigation and opening of the Data Analyzer.
  • URLParameter: Handles the creation and management of URL parameters.

Opening Data Analyzer from the Optimized Story

To open the Data Analyzer from an optimized story, the following scripting classes play a crucial role:

Example Scenario

Suppose a user wants to open the Data Analyzer by clicking on a table within the SAC story. The steps are as follows:

  1. Script on "Onclick" Function: Write the script for the table's Onclick function.
  2. Call NavigationUtils Class: Utilize this class to facilitate navigation.

 

URL Parameters

 

Creating URL parameters is essential for passing filters and variables. The URLParameter class is used for this purpose. Parameters to be specified include:

  • parameterName: Defines the parameter name to be passed, using specific naming patterns for filters and variables:
    • Filters: Use the pattern f<number>Dim (e.g., f01Dim for filter 1).
    • Variables: Use the pattern v<number>Par (e.g., v01Par for variable 1).
  • parameterValue: Corresponds to filter and variable values, matching the number in parameterName.

Example Code Snippet

 

 

NavigationUtils.openDataAnalyzer("<Connection Name>",  
                                 "<DataSource Name>",  
                                 [  
    UrlParameter.create("f01Dim","<Dimension Name>"), // filter 1  
    UrlParameter.create("f01Val","<Filter Value>"),   // filter 1 value  
    UrlParameter.create("f02Dim","<Dimension Name>"), // filter 2  
    UrlParameter.create("f02Val","<Filter Value>"),   // filter 2 value  
    UrlParameter.create("v01Par","<Variable ID>"),    // variable 1  
    UrlParameter.create("v01Val","<Variable Value>")  // variable 1 value  
                                 ],  
                                 true);

 

 

Parameters Explained

 

  • Connection: Specify the connection ID as a string.
  • dataSourceName: Provide the data source name, such as a Query name in case of BEx.
  • Parameters: Define URL parameters, including filters and variables.
  • newTab: Set to true to open in a new tab, or false to open in the same tab.

After running the story the URL created after the clicking the table will look like :

 

 

https://<tenant>#/dataanalyzer&/dz/?view_id=dataAnalyzer&connection=<Connection Name>&dataSourceName=<DataSource Name>&f01Dim=<Dimension Name>&f01Val=<Filter Value>&f02Dim=<Dimension Name>&f02Val=<Filter Value>&v01Par=<Variable ID>  &v01Val=<Variable Value>

 

 

Understanding URL Parameters

URL parameters in SAC are used to pass specific filters and variables from an Optimized Story to the Data Analyzer. This ensures that the Data Analyzer opens in the same state as the story, maintaining the user's context and selections.

Key URL Parameter Patterns

  • Filters:
    • Pattern: f<number>Dim and f<number>Val
    • Example:
      • f01Dim for the first filter dimension
      • f01Val for the first filter value
  • Variables:
    • Pattern: v<number>Par and v<number>Val
    • Example:
      • v01Par for the first variable parameter
      • v01Val for the first variable value

For more information regarding URL Parameters visit the following link :

SAP Help Portal | URL Parameters 

Conclusion

Passing URL parameters from an SAC Optimized Story to the Data Analyzer significantly enhances data exploration and user experience. By preserving the state of filters and variables, users can continue their analysis seamlessly. This scripting approach, leveraging SAC's powerful scripting classes, facilitates effective navigation and interaction between SAC components, empowering users to derive deeper insights.

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: Using "onAfterDataEntryProcess" event to implement Default Logic
entry.content:
A new table event “onAfterDataEntryProcess()” will be available with QRC1 2025 release in SAP Analytics Cloud. Please read this blog by my SAP colleague for details.
The “onAfterDataEntryProcess” event in SAP Analyti... - SAP 

 

We will be utilizing some simple scripting with a story using Optimized Design Experience. If you are not familiar with scripting, please check out these links.
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/unifying-story-and-analytic-application-with-the-new-optimized-story/ba-p/13570630

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/6a4db9a9c8634bcb86cecbf1f1dbbf8e.html

SAC Default Logic design flow

Detailed Steps to Implement

1. Create a new story using Optimized Design Experience

2. Add a table and layout rows, columns and filters as required to support planning entry

a. Change Id to T_Plan_Input

3. Add a button with description Clear Logs

a. Change Id to B_Clear

4. Add a button with description Calc Cost Centers #0

a. Change Id to B_DA_Run

5. Add a drop-down selection box

a. Change Id to DD_Cost_Centers

6. Add a Script Variable (under scripting)

a. Name: Changed_Cost_Centers | Type: string | Set as Array: On

7. Add a Data Action (under scripting)

a. Change Id: DA_RUN
b. Set default Version as required
c. Leave Cost Centers parameters blank

Sample layout of story

8. Edit onAfterDataEntryProcess script of T_Plan_Input table and add this code

If user makes changes to the same Cost Center in different rows, it will be added to the array only one time.
Line 8 captures the Cost Center Id only which the user can see using the Drop Down.

9. Edit onClick script of B_DA_Run button and add this code

10. Edit onClick script of B_Clear button and add this code

11. Save and close the story.

 

Configuration of Data Action Parameter

Data action using Parameter

Testing Story

1. Open Story

2. Make changes in rows for different Cost Centers as highlighted.

You will notice the button description has changed, now showing “Calc Cost Centers #2”
If you select the drop down, you can see a list of the Cost Center Id’s that have changed data.

3. Press the “Calc” button to execute the data action for only these two Cost Center’s.

The Completed message will show the number of Cost Centers that were calculated.

4. From the System menu, select Job Monitor and select the data action that was executed.

You will see the parameters that were passed to the data action for calculation.
The Cost Center description is shown in this window not the Cost Center Id.

Clear Logs

If the user wants to reset the change tracking for a planning session, they can

  1. Revert Data and
  2. Press the Clear Logs button, this will clear the global array.
  3. SAC will start tracking changes again with a clean slate.
